skill.name: infsh-cli
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code in using the inference.sh belt CLI to discover apps, run AI models, manage tasks, and handle authentication.
skill.summary: Use the inference.sh belt CLI to discover and run cloud AI apps from an AI assistant.
skill.author: infsh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns, but most are Markdown examples for a CLI documentation skill. The confirmed risks are legitimate but sensitive: pipe-to-shell installation guidance, cloud execution through belt commands, API key handling, and automatic local file uploads. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found, so publication is acceptable only with clear user warnings.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-to-Shell Installer Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents running a remote installer with curl piped directly to sh. This is a real supply-chain risk because users execute code fetched from the network without first inspecting it, although the same file also gives a manual checksum-based alternative.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is explicit in multiple files. Confidence is high, but this appears to be installer documentation rather than hidden malicious execution.

medium_findings.title: Cloud CLI Execution and External Service Calls
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to run belt commands that submit prompts, files, and tasks to inference.sh apps. This is expected behavior, but it can expose user content to external model providers and should require informed user consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and examples clearly show cloud task execution. The behavior is core functionality, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Local File Uploads
medium_findings.description: The documentation states that local paths are automatically uploaded when passed to URL-capable inputs. This can leak local images, audio, videos, or other files if an assistant selects paths without explicit user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload behavior is described directly and backed by examples with absolute, relative, and home-directory paths. The risk depends on user approval but is clearly real.

medium_findings.title: API Key Handling in Environment Variables
medium_findings.description: The skill documents use of INFSH_API_KEY for CI and troubleshooting. This is normal CLI authentication behavior, but it involves sensitive credentials that could be exposed by logs, prompts, or shell history.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact environment variable is documented and is clearly a credential. Confidence is high, but there is no evidence that the skill exfiltrates the value.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick and command execution alerts correspond to Markdown command examples such as belt app list, belt app run, and belt task get. They are not embedded executable Ruby code, but they still instruct agents and users to run external CLI commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected files are Markdown and the flagged lines are command examples or tables. This strongly supports a false positive for code execution, with residual risk from guided command use.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Checksum References
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings appear to come from mentions of SHA-256 checksums and model names, not from custom cryptographic implementation. I did not find evidence of weak hashing used for authentication or encryption.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The visible context does not show cryptographic code. Some static hits map to unrelated documentation text, so this finding is best treated as a false positive.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Product Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to inference.sh installer, documentation, image, and result URLs. These are expected for a CLI documentation skill and are not evidence of covert exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation and service endpoints matching the skill purpose. No suspicious third-party collection endpoint was found in the inspected files.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Alert Is a Relative Path Example
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged ../ in supported path examples. The file shows documentation of relative local file paths, not code that joins or reads attacker-controlled paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is plain documentation and contains no path parsing logic. It is a strong false positive for traversal, though local file upload still remains a separate privacy risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The install instructions include a command that downloads a remote script and pipes it to sh. Users should inspect the installer or use the documented manual checksum workflow before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact dangerous pattern is present and repeated. Its purpose is installation, not stealth execution, so it is not a block by itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Assistant-Callable External CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows Bash execution for belt commands and provides many examples for running remote AI apps. Misuse could submit private prompts or files to external services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools setting and examples confirm the pattern. The scope is limited to belt, reducing but not removing risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Path Upload Semantics
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI automatically uploads local files when file paths are provided. This pattern can become data exfiltration if paths are selected without user confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicitly documented and includes path examples. The risk is clear and directly relevant to user data exposure.

content.user_title: Run AI Apps with inference.sh CLI
content.value_statement: Teams need a quick way to discover and run hosted AI apps without managing GPUs or provider SDKs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through inference.sh CLI commands for apps, tasks, authentication, and file-based inputs.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install and authenticate the inference.sh belt CLI.
Lists commands for discovering, filtering, and inspecting available AI apps.
Shows how to run hosted image, video, audio, text, search, and 3D apps.
Documents task tracking commands for checking status and saving results.
Describes local file upload behavior for media inputs.
Provides references for app discovery, running apps, authentication, and CLI usage.
limitations: Requires the external belt CLI and an inference.sh account.
Can submit prompts, media, and files to cloud services when commands are run.
Does not verify app pricing, provider terms, or model availability in real time.
Does not implement its own model runtime or local GPU execution.

use_cases.title: Prototype AI Media Workflows
use_cases.description: Use belt commands to test image, video, audio, and 3D generation apps from a terminal workflow.

use_cases.title: Operate Hosted Model Tasks
use_cases.description: Discover apps, generate sample inputs, submit jobs, and retrieve results without writing provider-specific SDK code.

use_cases.title: Automate Research and Content Tasks
use_cases.description: Run search, LLM, and social automation apps through a consistent CLI interface after reviewing data sharing risks.

prompt_templates.title: Find Apps by Category
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the belt commands to find available inference.sh apps for image generation and inspect one promising option.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Simple Model Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me run an inference.sh app from the CLI using a short prompt, then explain how I can check the task result.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a File Upload Job
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe step-by-step plan for running an inference.sh app with a local media file. Ask me to confirm the file path before upload.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Repeatable CLI Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a repeatable inference.sh CLI workflow that discovers an app, saves a sample input, submits a long-running task, and records the result.

output_examples.input: Ask for image generation apps and a first command to inspect one option.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends listing image apps, narrowing by search terms, and inspecting a selected app before submitting any prompt or file.

output_examples.input: Ask how to run a long video generation task safely.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines sample input creation, user review of the prompt, no-wait submission, task status checks, and result retrieval.

output_examples.input: Ask how to use a local file with an app.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that local paths are uploaded and asks for explicit confirmation of the exact file before running the command.

best_practices: Inspect installer scripts or use the documented manual checksum workflow before installation.
Confirm every prompt, API key, and local file path before running belt commands.
Use generated sample inputs and app details to understand required fields before submitting tasks.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers to a shell in high-security environments without review.
Do not pass private files, secrets, or confidential prompts to hosted apps without approval.
Do not assume model availability, cost, or provider terms are unchanged without checking current app details.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps an assistant use the inference.sh belt CLI to discover apps, run tasks, authenticate, and track results.

faq.question: Does it run models locally?
faq.answer: No. It guides use of hosted inference.sh apps and external model providers through the CLI.

faq.question: Can it upload local files?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented CLI behavior uploads local paths used as app inputs, so file paths should be confirmed first.

faq.question: Does it need an API key?
faq.answer: Interactive login is documented, and INFSH_API_KEY can be used for scripts or CI workflows.

faq.question: Is the installer risk-free?
faq.answer: No. The skill documents a pipe-to-shell installer and a manual checksum workflow. Security-sensitive users should prefer manual review.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, with commands centered on the belt CLI.
